summary: Reclusive American poet known as the 'Belle of Amherst,' famous for her dashes, non-standard capitalization, and poems about death, nature, and the self. She corresponded with Thomas Wentworth Higginson and wrote the Master Letters to a mysterious addressee. Her poems were published posthumously by Mabel Loomis Todd.

works.name: Because I could not stop for Death
works.description: Dickinson's most famous poem, in which Death 'kindly stopped for me' in a carriage holding 'just Ourselves - / And Immortality.' The speaker 'passed the Fields of Gazing Grain' and 'the Setting Sun,' and 'first surmised the Horses' Heads / Were toward Eternity.' The speaker puts away 'My labor and my leisure too' for Death's 'Civility.'

clues.clue: Death 'kindly stopped for me' in a carriage with Immortality
examples: a figure who 'kindly stopped for me'
rides in a carriage that carries Immortality

clues.clue: 'Passed the Fields of Gazing Grain' and 'the Setting Sun'
examples: 'passed the Fields of Gazing Grain'
'the Setting Sun'

clues.clue: 'First surmised the Horses' Heads / Were toward Eternity'
examples: 'first surmised the Horses' Heads / Were toward Eternity'

works.name: I heard a Fly buzz - when I died
works.description: A poem imagining the speaker's own death, ending with 'I could not see to see.' The fly arrives with a 'Blue - uncertain - stumbling Buzz.' The speaker signs away 'What portion of me be / Assignable' and describes 'the Stillness in the Room / Was like the Stillness in the Air / Between the Heaves of Storm.' 'Breaths were gathering firm for that last Onset - when the King / Be witnessed.'

clues.clue: Ends with 'I could not see to see' after a fly's 'Blue - uncertain - stumbling Buzz'
examples: 'I could not see to see'
'Blue - uncertain - stumbling Buzz'

clues.clue: 'The Stillness in the Room / Was like the Stillness in the Air'
examples: 'the Stillness in the Room / Was like the Stillness in the Air'

clues.clue: Speaker signs away 'What portion of me be / Assignable'
examples: signing away 'What portion of me be / Assignable'

works.name: I felt a Funeral, in my Brain
works.description: A poem set inside the speaker's brain where 'Mourners, to and fro' keep treading. 'Boots of Lead' cause a creaking sound, 'all the Heavens were a Bell,' and 'a Plank in Reason, broke' causing the speaker to drop 'down, and down - And hit a World, at every plunge.'

clues.clue: 'Mourners, to and fro' tread in the brain; 'a Plank in Reason, broke'
examples: 'mourners, to and fro' that 'kept treading'
'a Plank in Reason, broke'

clues.clue: 'All the Heavens were a Bell'; speaker drops 'down, and down'
examples: 'all the Heavens were a Bell'
'dropped down' and 'hit a World'

works.name: There's a certain Slant of light
works.description: A poem about winter light that inflicts a 'Heavenly Hurt' with the 'Heft of Cathedral Tunes.' It was 'not Night' and 'not Frost' but 'tasted, like them all.' The light creates 'an imperial affliction' and when it goes, ''tis like the Distance / On the look of Death.'

clues.clue: Light with the 'Heft of Cathedral Tunes' inflicts a 'Heavenly Hurt'
examples: 'the Heft / of Cathedral Tunes'
inflicts a scarless 'heavenly hurt'

clues.clue: Describes a feeling 'not Night,' 'not Frost,' an 'imperial affliction'
examples: 'was not Night' and 'was not Frost'
'imperial affliction'

works.name: Hope is the thing with feathers
works.description: A poem describing hope as a bird that 'perches in the soul' and 'sings the tune without the words.' The speaker hears it 'in the chillest land - / And on the strangest Sea' and says 'sore must be the storm / That could abash the little Bird.'

clues.clue: Hope as a bird that 'perches in the soul' and 'sings the tune without the words'
examples: 'the tune without the words... that kept so many warm'
a bird who 'perches in the soul'

clues.clue: Heard 'in the chillest land - / And on the strangest Sea'
examples: hears the title entity 'in the chillest land'

works.name: I'm Nobody! Who are you?
works.description: A playful poem in which the speaker discovers a fellow 'Nobody' and warns 'Don't tell! They'd advertise - you know!' The poem asks 'How dreary - to be - Somebody!' and imagines an 'admiring Bog.'

clues.clue: 'Then there's a pair of us! / Don't tell!'; 'How dreary - to be - Somebody!'
examples: 'Then there's a pair of us! / Don't tell!'
'How dreary - to be - somebody!'

works.name: My Life had stood - a Loaded Gun
works.description: A poem in which the speaker is a loaded gun carried by an 'Owner' through 'Sovereign Woods.' When the speaker fires, 'the Mountains straight reply' with an echo. The speaker has 'the power to kill, / Without - the power to die.' The smile produces 'cordial light' likened to a 'Vesuvian face.' Adrienne Rich wrote about this poem in 'Vesuvius at Home.'

clues.clue: 'The power to kill, / Without - the power to die'; 'Mountains straight reply' when fired
examples: 'the power to kill / without the power to die'
'Mountains straight reply'

clues.clue: Smile produces 'cordial light' from a 'Vesuvian face'
examples: the 'cordial light' from the speaker's smile is likened to a 'Vesuvian face'

works.name: Wild Nights - Wild Nights!
works.description: An erotic poem with the lines 'Rowing in Eden!' and 'Done with the Compass - / Done with the Chart!' ending controversially with 'Might I but moor - tonight - / In Thee!' Susan Gilbert's name was erased from associated letters. The 2018 film Wild Nights with Emily by Madeleine Olnek is titled after this poem.

clues.clue: 'Rowing in Eden!'; 'Done with the Compass - / Done with the Chart!'; 'Might I but moor - tonight - / In Thee!'
examples: 'Done with the Compass - / Done with the Chart!'
'might I but moor tonight in thee!'

works.name: The Soul selects her own Society
works.description: A poem in which the soul 'shuts the Door - / To her divine Majority - / Present no more.' The soul 'selects her own society' and the speaker sees 'the Chariots - pausing - / At her low Gate.'

clues.clue: The soul 'shuts the Door' and 'selects her own society'; 'Chariots - pausing - / At her low Gate'
examples: 'selects her own society'
'shuts the Door - / To her divine Majority'

works.name: A narrow Fellow in the Grass
works.description: A riddle poem about a snake that leaves the speaker 'Zero at the Bone.' The Republican newspaper altered its punctuation, omitting a dash after 'his notice sudden is.' Susan Gilbert (Dickinson's sister-in-law) edited this poem.

clues.clue: A snake leaves the speaker 'Zero at the Bone'; the Republican altered its punctuation
examples: 'Zero at the Bone'
complained that The Republican's changes in punctuation altered the meaning

works.name: Master Letters
works.description: Three passionate, ecstatic letters addressed to a mysterious 'Master' figure, in which Dickinson refers to herself as 'Daisy.' One opens 'If you saw a bullet hit a Bird - and he told you he was'nt shot.' Possible addressees include Charles Wadsworth, Samuel Bowles, and Otis Lord. The first letter suggests walking with the dog Carlo. Lucie Brock-Broido titled a poetry collection after them.

clues.clue: Three passionate letters to a mysterious 'Master'; author calls herself 'Daisy'
examples: three ecstatic love letters addressed to a mysterious male figure
refers to herself in the third person as 'Daisy'

clues.clue: One opens 'If you saw a bullet hit a Bird'
examples: opens 'If you saw a bullet hit a Bird'

clues.clue: Possible addressees: Charles Wadsworth, Samuel Bowles, Otis Lord
examples: often theorized to be Otis Lord or Charles Wadsworth

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Dickinson lived reclusively in Amherst, Massachusetts, earning the nickname 'Belle of Amherst.' She corresponded with Thomas Wentworth Higginson after reading his Atlantic Monthly essay 'Letter to a Young Contributor,' sending four poems and asking if her 'Verse is alive.' Her sister Lavinia found over a hundred poems posthumously; Mabel Loomis Todd (who never met Dickinson) edited the first volumes, standardizing her dashes and capitalization. Thomas Johnson's 1955 edition restored the original punctuation. Susan Gilbert was Dickinson's sister-in-law with whom she had a potentially romantic relationship revealed by infrared technology. Dickinson's herbarium contained 424 pressed flower specimens. She allegedly only wore white cotton dresses. Films include A Quiet Passion (with Cynthia Nixon) and Dickinson (Apple TV+, with Hailee Steinfeld). Camille Paglia called her the American Marquis de Sade in Sexual Personae.

clues.clue: Nicknamed the 'Belle of Amherst'; lived reclusively in Amherst, Massachusetts

works.name: Other Poems
works.description: Other frequently clued poems include: 'After great pain, a formal feeling comes' (nerves sit 'ceremonious, like Tombs'); 'Tell all the truth but tell it slant' (truth must 'dazzle gradually'); 'A Bird came down the Walk' (bird bites an 'Angle Worm in halves'); 'I like to see it lap the miles' (a train riddle poem); 'Fame is a fickle food' and 'Fame is a bee' ('It has a sting'); 'I tasted a liquor never brewed' ('Debauchee of Dew'); 'They shut me up in Prose'; volcano poems connecting to Rich's 'Vesuvius at Home.'

clues.clue: 'After great pain, a formal feeling comes'; nerves sit 'ceremonious, like Tombs'; 'This is the Hour of Lead'
examples: 'After great pain, a formal feeling comes'

clues.clue: 'Tell all the truth but tell it slant' - truth must 'dazzle gradually / Or every man be blind'
examples: 'must dazzle gradually / Or every man be blind'

clues.clue: 'I tasted a liquor never brewed' - speaker is a 'Debauchee of Dew'
examples: 'tasted a liquor never brewed'

comprehensive_summary:
Emily Dickinson is one of the most heavily clued American poets, known for her reclusive life in Amherst, Massachusetts (the 'Belle of Amherst'), her distinctive dashes and non-standard capitalization, and her posthumous publication history.

'Because I could not stop for Death' is her most frequently giveaway poem: Death 'kindly stopped for me' in a carriage with Immortality, they 'passed the Fields of Gazing Grain' and 'the Setting Sun,' and the speaker 'first surmised the Horses' Heads / Were toward Eternity.' 'I heard a Fly buzz - when I died' ends with 'I could not see to see' after a 'Blue - uncertain - stumbling Buzz,' with 'the Stillness in the Room.' 'I felt a Funeral, in my Brain' features 'Mourners, to and fro,' 'Boots of Lead,' and 'a Plank in Reason, broke.'

'There's a certain Slant of light' inflicts a 'Heavenly Hurt' with the 'Heft of Cathedral Tunes.' 'Hope is the thing with feathers' describes a bird that 'perches in the soul' and 'sings the tune without the words.' 'My Life had stood - a Loaded Gun' declares 'the power to kill, / Without - the power to die,' with a 'Vesuvian face' smile. 'Wild Nights - Wild Nights!' contains 'Rowing in Eden!' and 'Done with the Compass.'

Dickinson's Master Letters -- three passionate letters to a mysterious figure in which she calls herself 'Daisy' -- are frequently clued. Her correspondence with Thomas Wentworth Higginson began when she asked 'are you too deeply occupied to say if my Verse is alive?' Her sister Lavinia found her poems after her death; Mabel Loomis Todd edited the first volumes, standardizing dashes. Thomas Johnson's 1955 edition restored original punctuation. Susan Gilbert was her sister-in-law with a potentially romantic relationship. Infrared technology revealed dedications had been removed. Her herbarium contained 424 pressed flowers. She allegedly only wore white. Adrienne Rich wrote 'Vesuvius at Home' about her. Billy Collins wrote 'Taking Off Emily Dickinson's Clothes.' Camille Paglia called her the American Marquis de Sade in Sexual Personae.
